creative.concept: Privacy audit: 'Where does your voice go?' two-column comparison card (cloud apps red / Jot white) + 5-slide carousel '5 things cloud dictation apps don't tell you'. Stops the scroll with a question that makes privacy-conscious users feel seen.

cards.title: Where does your voice go? — cloud vs Jot comparison
cards.use: Primary visual for LinkedIn and X. Export as 1080×1080 PNG. The red $8–15/mo on the left vs white $0 on the right is the scroll-stopper; the step-by-step flow makes the privacy difference concrete at a glance.

storyboard.title: Privacy revealed — choosing on-device dictation
storyboard.use: Instagram Reel / X video / GIF — privacy-angle hook for users who already question cloud apps; pairs with the privacy-audit card and carousel

shots.visual: Browser open to a cloud dictation app's Terms of Service; cursor highlights 'may retain recordings for model improvement'
Browser tab switches to Jot's GitHub repo README showing 'No telemetry. Audio never leaves the Mac.'
shots.overlay: Jot has no ToS for your voice.

shots.visual: Hand presses ⌥Space; Dynamic Island pill appears under Mac notch with pulsing waveform; user speaks
Full paragraph appears at cursor in Mail compose; pill vanishes
Wide Mac desktop shot; Jot icon in menu bar; jot-transcribe.com watermark lower-right

drafts.text:
Cloud dictation sends your voice to a server you've never seen, under terms of service you didn't read.

Wispr Flow: cloud-processed, $15/month.
Superwhisper cloud mode: same.

Jot transcribes entirely on your Mac's Neural Engine. Your voice never leaves. It's $0.

The privacy-first option ended up being the free one.

Would you pay $180/year for dictation if you knew a free, on-device alternative existed?

#macOS #privacy #indiedev
drafts.note: Attach privacy-audit.svg (export as 1080×1080 PNG). Do NOT put the link in the post body — LinkedIn downranks posts with external links. Paste the utm_url as the FIRST COMMENT immediately after posting. The closing question is the engagement trigger — answer every reply.

drafts.text:
Cloud dictation: your voice → their servers → their retention policy → $15/month.

Jot: hotkey → Neural Engine → text at cursor. Stays on your Mac. $0.
drafts.note: Attach privacy-audit.svg (export as PNG). No link in post — post the utm_url as the first reply immediately after posting. ~152 characters, within 200 limit.

drafts.text:
I realized cloud dictation apps were keeping my voice on their servers — so I built a free on-device alternative (I made this)

Six months ago I was paying for a Mac dictation app. Then I actually read the ToS.

Most cloud dictation apps retain your audio for model training. Duration varies but it's commonly 6–24 months. The audio crosses servers in data centers you've never seen. If there's a breach, an acquisition, or a policy change — your voice recordings go with it.

Medical notes. Work conversations. Personal thoughts. Anything you dictated.

So I built Jot and open-sourced it.

The core idea: transcription should run on your Mac, not on someone else's server. Jot uses Parakeet TDT (and Nemotron on higher-spec M2+ Macs) running on the Apple Neural Engine — the dedicated AI chip in every Apple Silicon Mac. No audio ever leaves the machine. Works completely offline after a one-time model download.

The workflow: press a hotkey (I use ⌥ Space), speak, transcript pastes wherever your cursor is in whatever app you're in. Mail, Slack, VS Code, Notion — any text field on macOS.

There's also:
- Optional AI cleanup/rewrite via Apple Intelligence (on-device, macOS 26+), Ollama, or your own API key
- Rewrite-with-voice: select text, press a hotkey, speak an instruction ("make this concise", "translate to Spanish"), Jot rewrites in place
- Custom vocabulary for product names, jargon, proper nouns
- An iOS companion app on the App Store

Apple Silicon only, macOS 14+. Free and open source.

(I built this — disclosure)

Link in comments if you want to try it.

drafts.text:
Cloud dictation: your voice → their servers.
Jot: your voice → your Mac. Done.

Free. On-device. Open source. Press a hotkey, speak, text at cursor. That's it.

Link in bio.

#macOS #privacy #dictation #voicetotext #speechtotext #opensource #macapps #indiedev

opportunities.title: Wispr Flow Alternatives — AlternativeTo.net
opportunities.why: AlternativeTo.net dominates search results for 'free mac dictation alternative'. Jot is not listed. A free submission takes 5 minutes and drives high-intent comparison-shopper traffic permanently.

opportunities.title: Superwhisper Alternatives — AlternativeTo.net
opportunities.why: Superwhisper alternatives pages appear in pages 2–8 of search results for 'superwhisper free alternative'. Jot as a free, open-source, on-device option is the single strongest answer to this query and is currently unlisted.

outreach.contact: Contact form at dictationdaddy.com (email unconfirmed — site returned 403; check site directly for contact page or email)
outreach.why: Published '5 Best Superwhisper Alternatives in 2026 (Reviewed)' — exactly the roundup where Jot belongs. As the only free, fully-open-source, on-device option, Jot fills the most obvious gap in that list.
outreach.pitch:
Hi,

Your '5 Best Superwhisper Alternatives in 2026' is one of the most useful breakdowns I've seen — the per-app breakdown of local vs cloud processing is exactly what people need to make a real decision.

I wanted to flag an app that should probably be on that list: Jot, which I built. It's a free, open-source Mac dictation app that runs entirely on-device on the Apple Neural Engine — no subscription, no account, no audio ever leaves the machine. Press a hotkey, speak, transcript pastes wherever your cursor is in any app.

It supports English, French, Spanish, German, Japanese, and more, with automatic model selection. It's the only fully-free on-device option I'm aware of in the Mac dictation space.

https://jot-transcribe.com/?utm_source=press&utm_medium=outreach&utm_campaign=dictationdaddy

Happy to provide screenshots, a demo video, or answer technical questions.

Vineet

outreach.contact: Contact form at macaiapps.com (email unconfirmed — site returned 403; check site directly for contact page or email)
outreach.why: Published 'Best Dictation Apps for macOS (2026): The Ultimate Guide to Voice-to-Text' — a high-SEO roundup article that appears in searches for the exact queries Jot should rank for. Being included here is worth more than a week of social posts.
outreach.pitch:
Hi,

Your 'Best Dictation Apps for macOS (2026): The Ultimate Guide to Voice-to-Text' is one of the most thorough breakdowns I've seen — the section on privacy tradeoffs between cloud and local processing is exactly the kind of detail that helps people make the right call.

I built Jot, a free open-source Mac dictation app I think belongs in that guide. Core loop: press a hotkey, speak, transcript pastes wherever your cursor is in any app. Transcription runs entirely on the Apple Neural Engine (Parakeet and Nemotron models) — no cloud, no account, no audio leaves the Mac. Optional AI rewrite via Apple Intelligence, Ollama, or user-provided API keys.

Apple Silicon, macOS 14+. Completely free. Open source on GitHub.

https://jot-transcribe.com/?utm_source=press&utm_medium=outreach&utm_campaign=macaiapps

Vineet
